cross_connections.description: Grandson and successor editor of the Springfield Republican — paper passed from grandfather to grandson through Sally Bowles + Thomas Hooker marriage.

cross_connections.display_label: Earlier abolitionist editor of The Emancipator from same Hooker tree

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1899, named for missionary grandfather
narrative_blocks.body: He was named for his paternal grandfather Rev. Richard Hooker, the Macon Georgia Presbyterian missionary who died at forty-nine in 1857. He graduated Yale in 1899 the same year his elder sister Aurelia died, and lived to age 89 in 1967.

narrative_blocks.header: Took over Springfield Republican from cousin Sam V
narrative_blocks.body: His maternal grandfather Samuel Bowles III had built the Springfield Republican into one of America's most influential newspapers from 1844 to 1878. When the fifth Samuel Bowles broke the three-generation editor pattern by not running the paper, Richard — his cousin and the son of Sally Bowles Hooker — took over as editor and general manager, carrying the newspaper from the Bowles family into the Hooker family.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Augusta Georgia, reared in New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: His Macon-born father Thomas Hooker was already settling toward a New Haven banking career when Richard was born at Augusta Georgia in 1878. The family soon settled at New Haven where Richard was reared and attended Yale, graduating with the class of 1899.

narrative_blocks.header: Washington correspondent before taking the Republican
narrative_blocks.body: After his year of European study he joined the Republican and served as its Washington correspondent from 1904 to 1911 — covering Roosevelt's late presidency and Taft's full term from the press galleries. He succeeded his grandfather Samuel Bowles III as publisher and editor in 1915.

narrative_blocks.header: Friend of Wilson, declined two presidential offers
narrative_blocks.body: He was a friend of President Woodrow Wilson and strongly supported him editorially during World War I and in the fight for the League of Nations. He declined Wilson's offer of Assistant Secretary of the Navy and an earlier offer from President Taft to serve as press secretary.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote Macmillan history of the Republican, 1924
narrative_blocks.body: He authored 'The Story of an Independent Newspaper' (Macmillan 1924), a history of his family's paper across nearly a century. He also wrote corporate histories of the Massachusetts Mutual and Aetna life insurance companies, drawing on his banking-family background.

narrative_blocks.header: Director of the Associated Press, 1927-1934
narrative_blocks.body: He served as a director of the Associated Press from 1927 to 1934, near the peak of his Republican editorial career. After stepping down from the Republican Company presidency in 1932 he contributed editorials almost until his death at eighty-nine in his Longmeadow MA home in 1967.

education.notes: Year of study in Europe after Yale graduation.

career.notes: Republican Washington correspondent 1904-1911.

Succeeded his maternal grandfather Samuel Bowles III as publisher and editor 1915. (Per 1934 Time magazine jingle, the 5th Samuel Bowles broke the 3-generation editor pattern — Richard, the Bowles cousin via the Hooker marriage, took over.)
President of the Republican Company until 1932; contributed editorials almost until his 1967 death.
Director of the Associated Press 1927-1934.

education.notes: Year of study in Europe after Yale graduation.

career.notes: Republican Washington correspondent 1904-1911.

Succeeded his maternal grandfather Samuel Bowles III as publisher and editor 1915. (Per 1934 Time magazine jingle, the 5th Samuel Bowles broke the 3-generation editor pattern — Richard, the Bowles cousin via the Hooker marriage, took over.)
President of the Republican Company until 1932; contributed editorials almost until his 1967 death.
Director of the Associated Press 1927-1934.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of a paleontologist who named a volcano
narrative_blocks.body: Her grandfather John Strong Newberry was a leading 19th-century American paleontologist and paleobotanist on the Columbia University School of Mines faculty for 24 years; he helped found the Geological Society of America. Two species — Eucyclogobius newberryi (goby) and Salmo newberryi (rainbow trout) — and Lake Newberry NY and Newberry Volcano in Oregon all bear his name.

burial.plot_notes: Alder Brook Cemetery, Guilford CT — funeral at Christ Episcopal Church Guilford

education.notes: Hawken School in Gates Mills, OH (the obit's 'Hawkins School in Gatesmills, OH' is a transcription of Hawken).
Yale Law 1938 — fourth straight generation in Hooker line at Yale (Pope Dwight; Tim V; HD0623 grandfather; father Richard Sr 1899; HD04884 himself JD 1938).

career.notes: Joined firm after Yale Law 1938; became partner. Firm dissolved in the 1980s.

career.organization: Thompson, Weir & Barclay → Murtha, Cullina, Richter & Pinney (merged)
career.notes: Became of counsel after Daggett Colby & Hooker dissolved; firm merged into Murtha Cullina; retired 1995.

institutions.blurb: Joined the firm 1938 after uncle Thomas Jr.'s 1936 death; preserved Hooker name as partner until 1980s dissolution.

narrative_blocks.header: Founding partner of Daggett, Colby & Hooker firm
narrative_blocks.body: After Williams 1935 and Yale Law 1938 he joined the New Haven law firm that would carry his name as Daggett, Colby & Hooker until its 1980s dissolution. He then went of counsel at Thompson Weir & Barclay, which later merged with Murtha Cullina Richter & Pinney; he retired 1995 after 57 years in law.

narrative_blocks.header: Society of Cincinnati member, fifty-seven year marriage
narrative_blocks.body: He held membership in the Society of the Cincinnati — the hereditary Revolutionary War officers' organization that his great-great-great-grandfather Pope Dwight had also belonged to. He married Mildred Bakewell in 1941 and the marriage lasted 57 years to his 1998 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Joined the firm his uncle had co-founded
narrative_blocks.body: His uncle Thomas Hooker Jr. had been name partner at White, Daggett and Hooker (1910), Bristol and White (1913-1934), and co-founder of Daggett and Hooker (1934) before his 1936 death. When Richard Jr. graduated Yale Law in 1938 he was admitted to partnership at the renamed Daggett, Colby and Hooker — preserving the family name in the firm across two generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for his great-grandfather Samuel Bowles editor
narrative_blocks.body: His middle name Bowles honored his maternal great-great-grandfather Samuel Bowles III — editor of the Springfield Republican 1844-1878. Born Cleveland OH 1925, he died Brooklyn NY at fifty-nine and was returned to the Longmeadow MA family cemetery for burial.

burial.plot_notes: Arlington National Cemetery — alongside her husband Dr. Ralph E. Knutti

marriages.officiant: Rev. Edwin Crocker, Blandford Congregational Church (at the Hooker summer home)

education.school_name: Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine

career.location: Rochester NY → Chicago IL → Appalachia region

career.role: Branch chief and Assistant Director, Office of Program Planning and Evaluation
career.organization: National Institute of Child Health and Human Development (NICHD)
career.notes: HEW Superior Service Medal 1971.

career.organization: Educational Council for Foreign Medical Graduates

career.notes: 25 years; retired 1993.

narrative_blocks.header: NICHD branch chief, HEW Superior Service Medal 1971
narrative_blocks.body: Vassar then Johns Hopkins MD then Rochester residency. She served as assistant director of Strong Memorial Hospital before becoming branch chief and assistant director of the Office of Program Planning and Evaluation at NICHD. The Department of Health Education and Welfare awarded her its Superior Service Medal in 1971.

narrative_blocks.header: Married NIH director Knutti at family summer home
narrative_blocks.body: After her first marriage to a Dr. Hardwicke ended in divorce, she married Dr. Ralph Eddy Knutti — then head of extramural programs at NIH's Arthritis and Metabolic Diseases Institute and soon to direct the National Heart Institute — on 17 August 1957 at the Hooker summer home in Blandford MA, with the Rev. Edwin Crocker officiating.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-five years in Coast Guard Auxiliary, retired 1993
narrative_blocks.body: She served 25 years in the U.S. Coast Guard Auxiliary qualifying in search and rescue and communications, retiring in 1993 at age 80. She and Ralph were buried together at Arlington National Cemetery — the only Hookers in the tree interred there.

burial.plot_notes: Arlington National Cemetery — alongside his wife Dr. Sarah Hooker Knutti

education.school_name: Western Reserve University School of Medicine

career.organization: University of Rochester School of Medicine

career.notes: 9 years at USC.

career.organization: NIH National Institute of Arthritis and Metabolic Diseases
career.notes: Commissioned in Public Health Service officer corps 1951; pioneered fellowship and grant programs at the new Institute.

career.organization: NIH National Institute of Arthritis and Metabolic Diseases

career.notes: Director 1961-1965 — one of NIH's two largest Institutes. Asked by Dr. James Shannon to move from Arthritis Institute to head NHI.

narrative_blocks.header: Helped create NIH's Arthritis Institute, 1951
narrative_blocks.body: After the 1951 Omnibus Medical Research Bill created three new NIH Institutes, he was asked to come to Bethesda to help build the National Institute of Arthritis and Metabolic Diseases as its first Chief of Extramural Programs. He pioneered the fellowship and grant programs that became the model for NIH-wide extramural research support.

narrative_blocks.header: Directed National Heart Institute, 1961-1965
narrative_blocks.body: In 1961 NIH director Dr. James Shannon asked him to move to direct the National Heart Institute — now the National Heart, Lung and Blood Institute. He led one of NIH's two largest Institutes for four years before stepping down in 1965.

cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, the famed Springfield publisher

bio.notes: Named "Thomas" — honoring the founding ancestor Thomas Hooker (H00001)

narrative_blocks.header: Carried Thomas Hooker's blood through both parents
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Macon Georgia in September 1849 to Rev. Richard Hooker and Aurelia Dwight, he was the third documented child of a Hooker-Hooker cousin marriage in the tree — and the first whose dual descent propagated biologically. Through his father he descended directly from Thomas Hooker via the John Hooker line of Northampton; through his mother via Mary Hooker Pierpont's 1690s marriage and the Edwards-Dwight succession to Pope of Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Macon-born, married a Springfield Republican daughter
narrative_blocks.body: He was born at Macon Georgia in September 1849, the only child of a Presbyterian minister and a Dwight bride. In 1874 he married Sarah Augusta 'Sally' Bowles of Springfield Massachusetts — daughter of Samuel Bowles III, the editor of the Springfield Republican newspaper, then one of America's most influential dailies.

narrative_blocks.header: Both sons graduated Yale, younger took Harvard Law
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Aurelia died at twenty-four in 1899; his elder son Richard graduated Yale in 1899 the same year of her death; his younger son Thomas Jr graduated Yale in 1903 and went on to Harvard Law in 1906. He himself attended Yale's Sheffield Scientific School for two years without completing the degree.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-five years in New Haven banking and trusts
narrative_blocks.body: Returning to New Haven in 1877 after five years of study and travel abroad, he entered business and financial life. He became vice president of the New Haven Trust Company in 1895, its president from 1902 to 1909, and at the merger that created Union & New Haven Trust Company he stepped to vice president there — while simultaneously rising at the First National Bank to vice president 1902, president 1909.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with mother Aurelia, wife Sally, and Pope Dwight forebears

education.notes: Hopkins Grammar School in New Haven for early education — same school where his Dwight great-great-grandfather Pope Dwight had taught after his 1769 Yale graduation.
Yale MA 1872 — three years after BA.
Five years 1872-1877 of study and travel abroad and in this country; returned to New Haven 1877 to enter business and financial career.

career.notes: Vice president of New Haven Trust Company 1895-1902.
Joined First National Bank 1895.
President of New Haven Trust Company 1902-1909, until its merger into Union & New Haven Trust Company.
VP First National Bank 1902-1909.
President of First National Bank from 1909 — held position at time of Carrington 1918 biography.
VP of Union & New Haven Trust Company from 1909 (after merger of NH Trust Co).
Ten years on New Haven Board of Education, 1894-1904.

parents.father_research_notes: Now structurally wired to X02841 Samuel Bowles III Easter Egg.
parents.mother_research_notes: Now structurally wired to X02842 Mary Bowles Easter Egg.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT, with husband Thomas Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of the most famous American editor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Samuel Bowles III edited the Springfield Republican newspaper into one of the most influential American dailies of the Civil War and Reconstruction eras. She grew up in the publisher's household and married Thomas Hooker at Springfield on 30 June 1874 at age twenty-four.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost daughter Aurelia young, raised two Yale sons
narrative_blocks.body: She bore three children in New Haven between 1875 and 1882. Her daughter Aurelia died at twenty-four in 1899 — the same year her elder son Richard graduated Yale; her younger son Thomas Jr followed at Yale in 1903 and took Harvard Law in 1906.

education.notes: Two-year special course, Sheffield Scientific School

notable.notable_blurb: uncle to the medical-textbook author Dr. Worthington Hooker

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, Hampden County MA — returned to family burial ground

education.notes: Yale 1827 — youngest of the four Hooker brothers to attend Yale (John 1810, George 1814, Worthington 1825, Richard 1827).

career.notes: Settled in Macon Georgia after Yale and ordination; ministered there for several decades. His son Thomas was born at Macon in 1849.

narrative_blocks.header: Headed south after Yale, settled in Macon
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale 1827 he was ordained Presbyterian minister and settled in Macon, Bibb County, Georgia — the southernmost outpost of any Hooker brother of his generation. His three older brothers stayed close to Connecticut and Massachusetts in medicine and law; Richard took the gospel south.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Pope of Yale's granddaughter at thirty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: On 15 July 1846 he married Aurelia Dwight at New Haven CT — daughter of James Dwight III and granddaughter of Rev. Timothy Dwight IV, the eighth president of Yale College. The marriage came eighteen years after his Yale graduation and uncle Worthington's later Yale professorship.

narrative_blocks.header: Their son carried two Hooker lines at once
narrative_blocks.body: Their only child Thomas was born at Macon GA in September 1849 — a dual-line Hooker descendant through both parents. From his father Thomas inherited the direct John Hooker descent; from his mother, descent via Mary Hooker's marriage to Rev. James Pierpont in 1690s New Haven, through Sarah Pierpont's marriage to theologian Jonathan Edwards, through Mary Edwards Dwight to Pope of Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-nine in Middletown, buried home
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Middletown, Connecticut on 19 December 1857 at age forty-nine, while north on a visit from Macon. His body was returned to Springfield Cemetery in Hampden County MA to lie with his Hooker parents and Dwight grandparents.

narrative_blocks.header: Posthumously, brother-in-law became Yale's twelfth president
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Aurelia's younger brother Timothy Dwight V was eighteen at the time of Richard's 1846 marriage to her and twenty-nine when Richard died in 1857. Twenty-nine years after Richard's death, Timothy became the twelfth President of Yale in 1886 — the Hooker-Dwight family's second Yale presidency in three generations after Pope Dwight 1795-1817.

parents.mother_name: Susan (Breed) Dwight of New Haven CT

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with parents and Pope of Yale grandfather

narrative_blocks.header: Pope of Yale was her grandfather
narrative_blocks.body: Her father James Dwight III was the third son of Rev. Timothy Dwight IV — the eighth President of Yale College, known to his contemporaries as 'Pope Dwight.' Through her great-grandmother Mary Edwards Dwight (daughter of theologian Jonathan Edwards) she also carried the Yale Edwards line back to Northampton.

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage united two Hooker descents
narrative_blocks.body: Her 1846 marriage to Rev. Richard Hooker brought together two seventh- and eighth-generation descendants of Thomas Hooker through entirely different lines — Richard via direct paternal John Hooker descent, Aurelia via Mary Hooker's 1690s marriage to Rev. James Pierpont and the subsequent Edwards-Dwight succession.

narrative_blocks.header: Survived husband by seventeen years
narrative_blocks.body: Widowed at forty-one in December 1857 when Richard died at Middletown, she returned to her New Haven family home and lived another seventeen years. She died there in January 1874 at fifty-seven and was buried at Grove Street Cemetery near her Dwight grandparents and parents.

death.cause: Stroke (second major stroke; first in December 1877)

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, Hampden County MA — with wife Mary, who outlived him by 15 years

parents.father_name: Samuel Bowles Jr. II (1797-1851), founder of the Springfield Weekly Republican 1824
parents.father_research_notes: His father started the Republican as a weekly in 1824, ran it until his death in 1851 — when 25-year-old Samuel III took over entire management.

education.notes: Attended from age 13. Wanted to attend college but his father deemed college unnecessary for printer training (and likely couldn't afford it). Bowles regretted not attending college his entire life — and educated all his own children.

career.notes: Convinced father to launch daily edition 1844 (first issue 27 March 1844); took over entire newspaper at father's 1851 death at age 25. Under his leadership the Republican gained national reputation as 'the best and ablest country journal published on the continent' (NY Tribune). Pioneer of independent journalism.
Invested $10,000 and resigned from the Republican to take Boston Traveller editorship; resigned after 4 months when project's owners proved disunited and undercapitalized.
Trustee of Amherst College for the last 12 years of his life. Visits to The Evergreens (home of Susan and Austin Dickinson in Amherst) were associated with these trustee meetings.

cross_connections.description: Grandson who succeeded him at the Springfield Republican generations later — the newspaper passed from grandfather Samuel III to grandson Richard via Sally's 1874 marriage to Thomas Hooker.
Close friend of Emily Dickinson; received 40+ of her poems and 50+ letters; she called his presence 'a creator of endless perspectives' (via her sister-in-law Susan Dickinson).

cross_connections.display_label: Both Republican Party founders (Bowles MA 1855; Anthony IL)
the Hooker who married his daughter Sally

narrative_blocks.header: Took over the paper at twenty-five
narrative_blocks.body: He convinced his father in 1844 to launch a daily edition of the Springfield Weekly Republican — the first issue ran 27 March 1844 with no subscribers and no advertisers, losing two hundred dollars that first year. When his father died in 1851 the twenty-five-year-old Samuel inherited the entire paper, which under his leadership grew to dominate New England regional journalism.

narrative_blocks.header: Helped found the Massachusetts Republican Party, 1855
narrative_blocks.body: Initially conservative on slavery — he had supported the Fugitive Slave Act — he switched during the Kansas-Nebraska debates and became an abolitionist. He headed efforts to create a Republican Party in Massachusetts in 1855, was one of the first editors to support Frémont in 1856, and was a delegate to the 1860 Chicago convention that nominated Lincoln.

narrative_blocks.header: Received forty poems from Emily Dickinson
narrative_blocks.body: Beginning around 1858 he became a close friend of Susan and Austin Dickinson at The Evergreens in Amherst, where he met Austin's sister Emily — the poet. Over the next decade she sent him approximately forty poems and fifty letters; Susan Dickinson called him 'a creator of endless perspectives.' Some scholars consider him a candidate for Dickinson's 'Master' figure.

narrative_blocks.header: Jailed in NYC over robber-baron Fisk editorial
narrative_blocks.body: He published a scathing editorial about Erie Railroad director and robber baron James Fisk. Fisk retaliated by using his Tammany Hall connections to have Bowles arrested on trumped-up charges during a visit to New York City; Bowles spent time in jail and on returning home to Springfield wrote about Fisk again.

narrative_blocks.header: Fired own brother, never reconciled before stroke
narrative_blocks.body: In 1875 he fired his younger brother Benjamin Franklin Bowles from the paper's counting room despite competent management — for not meeting Samuel's exacting standard. Benjamin moved to Paris and died the next year; Samuel had a major stroke December 1877 and died in January 1878 from a second, never having reconciled with his brother or his Congressman brother-in-law.

sources.label: Emily Dickinson Museum — Samuel Bowles friendship page

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, Hampden County MA — with husband Samuel III

parents.father_name: Henry VanRensselaer Schermerhorn (1797-1870) — prominent NY Dutch family
parents.mother_name: Hannah Buckminster Dwight Schermerhorn (1806-1838) — daughter of James Scutt Dwight 1769-1822 + Mary Sandford Dwight 1774-1844; this is a separate Dwight branch from the Pope of Yale family
parents.mother_research_notes: Her mother died when Mary was 11. Her maternal grandfather James S. Dwight was 'a leading merchant in Springfield in the early 19th century' per Samuel Bowles III Wikipedia biography.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of seven, friend of Emily Dickinson
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Geneva NY to a Schermerhorn-Dwight household, she married Samuel Bowles III in 1848 and bore seven children, one of whom (Sally) married Thomas Hooker in 1874. She corresponded warmly with Emily Dickinson alongside her husband and outlived Samuel by fifteen years.

burialCemetery.notes: Longmeadow Cemetery in the long-time Hooker family town of Longmeadow, Hampden County MA. Burial site of Arthur Bowles Hooker (HD04885), son of Richard Hooker the Springfield Republican editor.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
